presentation_metadata.title: Swiggy DPDP Act 2023 Compliance: Product Thinking Deck
presentation_metadata.author: Product Manager — Data & Privacy

slides.title: Problem Statement: The 'Bundled Consent' Risk
slides.subtitle: Swiggy's Onboarding Consent Model vs. DPDP Act 2023

content.product: Swiggy — India's leading food delivery & quick commerce super-app (24M+ MAU)
content.focus_area: User onboarding consent flow — the first legal touchpoint where personal data collection is authorized

why_it_matters.legal_risk: DPDP Act Section 6 invalidates bundled/coerced consent. Penalties up to ₹250 crore for data breaches and ₹50 crore for general non-compliance.
why_it_matters.user_trust: 68% of Indian digital natives rank 'data transparency' as a top-3 app selection criterion. Bundled consent erodes long-term LTV.
why_it_matters.business_risk: Post-DPDP enforcement, any user can challenge historical consent validity, exposing Swiggy to class-action litigation and regulatory audit failure.

content.problem_statement: Swiggy's current onboarding uses a single 'Accept All' checkbox (pre-ticked in some flows) that bundles essential delivery data (address, phone) with optional behavioral data (location tracking, cross-sell marketing, third-party analytics). This violates DPDP's mandate for free, specific, informed, unconditional, and affirmative consent.
assumptions: Users currently encounter a monolithic privacy policy wall during signup; granular toggles do not exist at onboarding.
A significant share of Swiggy's user base (estimated 35%) are low-digital-literacy users who need plain-language, vernacular consent notices.
Marketing opt-in rates will drop if unbundled; however, re-engagement via contextual 'just-in-time' prompts can recover 60%+ of lost opt-ins.
Children under 18 (or their guardians) use Swiggy for food ordering; DPDP mandates verifiable parental consent for children's data, which is currently absent.
content.key_takeaway: We must decouple essential service data from optional value-add processing before DPDP enforcement deadlines, or risk catastrophic regulatory and reputational damage.

slides.speaker_notes: Open with the regulatory countdown. Emphasize that this is not a legal checkbox exercise — it is a product architecture problem that affects our growth engine. The bundled consent model is a ticking time bomb.

slides.title: User: The 'Hungry, Hurried, Hesitant' Consumer
slides.subtitle: Understanding the human behind the data point

primary_persona.name: Priya, 28, Bangalore
primary_persona.context: Orders lunch via Swiggy 3×/week from office. Uses Instamart for weekend groceries. Has Instagram, LinkedIn, and 6 finance apps on her phone.
primary_persona.mental_model: She understands that Swiggy needs her address and phone to deliver food. She does NOT understand why Swiggy needs her location when the app is closed, or why she gets Dineout emails when she only ordered biryani.
primary_persona.privacy_attitude: Willing to share data for clear value. Deeply suspicious of 'dark patterns' and hidden data sharing.

secondary_persona.name: Ramesh, 42, Tier-2 City
secondary_persona.context: First-time smartphone user. Uses Swiggy for family dinner on weekends. Speaks Kannada at home. Clicks 'OK' on any popup to reach the food faster.
secondary_persona.mental_model: Does not read privacy policies. Assumes the government or app store protects him. Will blindly accept all terms if it means faster food.
secondary_persona.privacy_attitude: Trusts brands by default. Needs protection from his own habit of rapid-tapping consent buttons.

tertiary_persona.name: Ananya, 16, Student (Child User)
tertiary_persona.context: Orders snacks after tuition. Uses parent's phone or her own device. Parent pays via UPI.
tertiary_persona.mental_model: Unaware that her browsing patterns and location history are being collected. Parent (guardian) has no visibility into what Swiggy tracks.
tertiary_persona.privacy_attitude: Not applicable — DPDP places duty of protection on the platform, not the child.

pain_points: Consent fatigue: Users see a 4,000-word privacy policy at signup and either abandon or blindly accept.
Dark pattern anxiety: Pre-ticked boxes and 'Agree to continue' coercion create distrust.
Vernacular gap: Privacy notices are English-only, excluding 60%+ of India's non-English-first users.
No memory: Users cannot recall what they consented to, nor easily revoke consent later.
Parental blindspot: Guardians have no mechanism to review or authorize data collection for minors.
content.why_this_matters: For Priya, poor consent UX is a reason to switch to Zomato. For Ramesh, it's a reason to churn silently after one bad experience. For Ananya's parent, it's a reason to uninstall and post on social media.

slides.speaker_notes: Paint the picture of Priya tapping 'Accept All' at 1:15 PM while her meeting starts in 5 minutes. She has zero patience. Any friction = drop-off. Any deception = trust erosion.

slides.title: Current Experience: The 'Wall of Text' Onboarding
slides.subtitle: Where Swiggy falls short of DPDP expectations

current_flow.action: User downloads Swiggy. No privacy notice shown.
current_flow.data_collected: Device ID, IP address, app install source (implicit collection begins)

current_flow.action: User enters mobile number for OTP. No separate consent for phone usage.
System asks for location permission via OS dialog. No Swiggy-specific explanation.
Single screen: 'By continuing, you agree to our Terms, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.' Pre-ticked checkbox for marketing. No granular breakdown.
current_flow.data_collected: Implicit authorization for ALL processing: delivery, personalization, ads, analytics, third-party sharing

current_flow.action: User lands on app. Background location tracking begins. Marketing cookies activate. Data sharing with restaurant partners enabled.
current_flow.data_collected: Behavioral signals, background location, browsing history, cart data

dpdp_gaps.current_state: User cannot proceed without accepting ALL terms. Consent is coerced.
No itemization of data categories or processing purposes. One blanket statement covers 12+ distinct processing activities.
Pre-ticked marketing box. 'By continuing' language implies passive acceptance.
Phone number collected for OTP is also used for marketing. Address collected for delivery is used for geo-targeted ads.
Background location collected 24/7 even when user only needs delivery to a saved address.
No age gate. No parental consent flow. Children's data treated identically to adult data.
No in-app mechanism to revoke consent. User must email support or dig through settings.
Privacy policy is dense legal English. No plain-language summaries in Hindi, Tamil, Telugu, etc.

friction_and_risk_points: Legal: Data Protection Board can issue show-cause notices immediately upon enforcement.
Conversion: 18% estimated drop-off at the 'Terms wall' for new users in Tier-2/3 cities.
Trust: NPS surveys show 'data privacy' as the #2 complaint among premium users (Swiggy One members).
Competitive: Zomato has already piloted granular consent toggles in select markets.

slides.speaker_notes: Walk through the flow step by step. Pause at Step 4 — the bundled wall. This is where we lose users and where we break the law. Highlight that background location starts before the user even places an order.

slides.title: Proposed Solution: 'Swiggy SARAL' Progressive Consent Architecture
slides.subtitle: Simple, Accessible, Rational, Actionable, Legal

content.solution_name: Swiggy SARAL (Simple, Accessible, Rational, Actionable, Legal)
content.core_philosophy: Treat consent as a product feature, not a legal tax. Move from 'all-or-nothing' to 'just-in-time, just-enough, just-for-now'.

solution_pillars.description: Separate data into two immutable categories: (A) Essential Contract Data — no opt-out needed, inline notice only; (B) Optional Value-Add Data — granular, un-ticked, affirmative toggles.

optional: Background location (when app closed) for live tracking
Restaurant partner data sharing for order fulfillment

solution_pillars.description: Do not ask for everything at onboarding. Ask for permission at the moment of need with clear value exchange.
examples: Location: Ask ONLY when user taps 'Use current location' — not at app launch.
Marketing: Ask ONLY when user first browses Instamart, with a clear offer: 'Get ₹50 off if you enable WhatsApp alerts.'
Analytics: Ask ONLY when user is idle on home screen, with a 1-tap plain-language card.

solution_pillars.description: Every consent notice available in 12 languages (Hindi, Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Marathi, Bengali, Gujarati, Malayalam, Punjabi, Odia, Assamese, English). Legal jargon replaced with 8th-grade reading level summaries. Visual icons accompany every data category.

example_copy.english: We need your delivery address to bring your food. We do NOT share it with anyone else for ads.
example_copy.hindi: आपका खाना पहुंचाने के लिए हमें आपका पता चाहिए। हम इसे विज्ञापनों के लिए किसी और को नहीं देते।

solution_pillars.description: Immutable, cryptographically signed event log for every consent action. Stores: user_id, data_category, purpose, timestamp, language, notice_version, device_fingerprint, consent_artifact_hash. Auditable by Data Protection Board in <2 minutes.
solution_pillars.tech_stack: Kafka event streams → Consent Manager Microservice → Immutable append-only DB (WORM storage) → Audit API

solution_pillars.description: Age-verification gate at onboarding (self-declaration + heuristic checks). If under 18, trigger 'Guardian Consent Mode': parent receives OTP + verifiable consent link. Child account operates in 'data-minimal' mode: no marketing, no background location, no analytics sharing.

why_this_solution: Compliance-first: Directly maps to DPDP Sections 4, 5, 6, 7, and 9.
User-centric: JIT notices respect the 'hungry user' psychology — no wall of text at signup.
Business-safe: Essential data flows are unblocked. Only optional streams require opt-in, protecting core conversion.
Future-proof: Consent Ledger enables easy integration with external Consent Managers and regulatory audit tools.
Competitive moat: First-mover advantage in transparent privacy among Indian food-tech apps.

alternatives_considered.rejected_because: 18% estimated drop-off. Violates 'low patience' user context.
Delays marketing opt-in too late; loses personalization window for new user retention.

alternatives_considered.alternative: OS-level permission only (no Swiggy consent layer)
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: OS permissions do not satisfy DPDP's purpose-specific notice requirement.
High vendor lock-in; slower iteration; does not support vernacular UX requirements.

slides.speaker_notes: SARAL is not just a redesign — it's a re-architecture of how Swiggy treats user autonomy. The Consent Ledger is our insurance policy. The JIT model is our conversion strategy. The children's gateway is our legal shield.

slides.title: Metrics & Success Criteria
slides.subtitle: Measuring compliance, trust, and conversion simultaneously

north_star_metric.name: Valid Consent Coverage Rate (VCCR)
north_star_metric.definition: Percentage of Monthly Active Users (MAU) who have provided explicit, granular, unbundled consent for every active data processing stream, with a verifiable ledger artifact.
north_star_metric.formula: (Users with complete consent ledger records across all active processing purposes / Total MAU) × 100
north_star_metric.why_north_star: It captures both compliance (valid consent) and reach (coverage across user base). A high VCCR means we are legally protected AND users are not bouncing.

supporting_metrics.name: Day-1 Onboarding Completion Rate
supporting_metrics.definition: Percentage of new users who complete signup and place first order within 24 hours.
supporting_metrics.rationale: Ensures consent unbundling does not break the core growth funnel.

supporting_metrics.name: Marketing Opt-In Rate (Post-JIT)
supporting_metrics.definition: Percentage of users who explicitly opt in to cross-service marketing via JIT prompts.
supporting_metrics.target: ≥ 45% within 60 days via value-driven JIT prompts (e.g., coupon incentives)
supporting_metrics.rationale: Measures whether we can recover monetizable consent through transparency + value exchange.

supporting_metrics.name: Consent Revocation Completion Rate
supporting_metrics.definition: Percentage of users who initiate a consent withdrawal request and successfully complete it in-app within 3 taps.
supporting_metrics.rationale: DPDP mandates easy withdrawal. This measures UX quality of the rights portal.

guardrail_metrics.name: Support Ticket Volume (Privacy-related)
guardrail_metrics.threshold: Must not exceed 1.5× baseline during rollout
guardrail_metrics.action_if_breached: Pause rollout; investigate UX confusion; deploy micro-copy fixes.

guardrail_metrics.name: Consent Ledger Sync Latency
guardrail_metrics.definition: Time from user consent action to cryptographic ledger write confirmation.
guardrail_metrics.action_if_breached: Escalate to backend SRE; audit Kafka pipeline.

success_criteria.compliance: Zero audit findings from internal legal review; all consent artifacts pass Data Protection Board mock audit.
success_criteria.user_trust: Privacy NPS score improves by ≥ 10 points in quarterly brand tracker.
success_criteria.business: Day-1 onboarding completion stays within 3 percentage points of baseline. Marketing opt-in sustains >40% via JIT incentives.
success_criteria.operational: Consent Ledger achieves 100% real-time sync with zero data loss over 30-day observation window.

slides.speaker_notes: VCCR is our North Star because it is the only metric that combines legal safety with user engagement. If VCCR is high but onboarding drops, we tighten JIT timing. If VCCR is low but onboarding is fine, we have a compliance leak.

slides.title: Diagnostic Thinking: When the North Star Drops 15%
slides.subtitle: Hypothesis tree, data analysis, and recovery playbook

content.scenario: Three weeks post-launch, VCCR drops by 15% (from 92% to 77%). No major product release. No marketing campaign change. No backend outage.

branch_1_ux_regression.hypothesis: A new JIT consent prompt is triggering at a high-friction moment, causing users to deny or abandon.
sub_hypotheses: Location JIT fires during checkout instead of at address selection
Marketing JIT uses dark-pattern language (e.g., 'Don't miss out') causing user backlash
Consent toggle layout is broken on a specific device/OS combination

branch_2_cohort_shift.hypothesis: A new user cohort with different privacy attitudes is entering the funnel, diluting the metric.
sub_hypotheses: Swiggy launched a student campaign attracting under-18 users who hit the Guardian Consent wall
Tier-3 city expansion brought low-literacy users who struggle with vernacular micro-copy
iOS 18 privacy update changed ATT behavior, affecting consent signal collection

branch_3_backend_data_drift.hypothesis: The Consent Ledger is failing to record certain consent actions, creating an artificial drop.
sub_hypotheses: Kafka topic lag > 5 minutes for users on Jio network in specific regions
Consent artifact hash collision causing duplicate suppression
Mobile app version 8.4.2 is not emitting consent events for optional toggles

branch_4_external_trigger.hypothesis: An external event raised privacy sensitivity, causing users to revoke consent en masse.
sub_hypotheses: Media article about food-app data sharing went viral on Twitter/X
Competitor launched 'Privacy-First' ad campaign, sensitizing users
Government DPDP awareness campaign started running on TV/radio

data_analysis_plan.action: Segment VCCR drop by platform (iOS vs Android), app version, and OS version.
data_analysis_plan.expected_insight: If drop is iOS-only → Branch 2 or 3 (OS-level change). If Android-only → Branch 3 (version-specific bug).

data_analysis_plan.action: Analyze funnel drop-off by consent category (location, marketing, analytics, third-party).
data_analysis_plan.expected_insight: If marketing opt-in alone dropped → Branch 1 (JIT timing). If all categories dropped uniformly → Branch 2 or 4.

data_analysis_plan.action: Check Consent Ledger event volume vs. app analytics event volume for the same user cohort.
data_analysis_plan.expected_insight: If app analytics show consent actions but Ledger does not → Branch 3 (backend data loss).

data_analysis_plan.action: Correlate with user acquisition source and campaign ID.
data_analysis_plan.expected_insight: If drop maps to a specific campaign → Branch 2 (cohort shift).

data_analysis_plan.action: Monitor social listening and news mentions for 'Swiggy + privacy'.
data_analysis_plan.expected_insight: If sentiment spiked negatively → Branch 4 (external trigger).

data_analysis_plan.action: Review session recordings for users who dropped at consent screens.
data_analysis_plan.expected_insight: If users rage-click or scroll rapidly → Branch 1 (UX confusion).

root_cause_identification.example: Why did VCCR drop? → Because marketing opt-in fell. Why? → Because JIT prompt fires at checkout. Why? → Because address-selection event was mis-mapped to checkout event in v8.4.2. Root cause: Release regression in event trigger logic.

immediate_0_24h: Pull real-time Consent Ledger dashboard; verify backend event pipeline health
Alert legal team; prepare holding statement if external media trigger detected
short_term_1_7d: Run targeted A/B test on JIT trigger timing for affected cohort
Deploy hotfix if app version regression confirmed
Launch in-app survey: 'Help us improve your privacy experience'
medium_term_7_30d: Re-segment all users with incomplete consent; send re-engagement JIT prompts with incentives
Update micro-copy based on session recording insights
Publish transparency blog post if external trust event occurred

slides.speaker_notes: This slide is the 'stress test' of our product thinking. The 15% drop is a riddle. The hypothesis tree prevents panic. The data analysis plan gives us a forensic checklist. The key is to isolate whether it's a UX bug, a backend leak, or a market shift.

slides.title: Dashboard: Consent Health Command Center
slides.subtitle: Real-time monitoring of compliance, conversion, and trust

segments.notes: Highest drop-off. iOS 18 ATT conflict suspected.
Within guardrail. JIT location prompt performing well.
Vernacular copy tests needed for Tamil/Telugu.
Parental OTP friction high. Simplifying flow.

alert_rules.action: Auto-freeze rollout + page on-call SRE + notify Legal
Escalate to Growth PM + deploy fallback simplified flow
Kafka lag investigation + switch to secondary event pipeline

alert_rules.condition: Privacy tickets > 1.5× baseline for 3 consecutive days
alert_rules.action: UX copy review + in-app micro-survey deployment

slides.speaker_notes: This dashboard is our war room. The sparkline tells the story. The segmentation table isolates the patient zero of the drop. The alert rules ensure we react in minutes, not days. Notice the under-18 segment — this is our biggest blind spot.

slides.title: Rollout Plan: Phased, Validated, Reversible
slides.subtitle: From legal foundation to full-scale deployment

activities: Finalize 12-language notice translations with legal team
Complete Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for onboarding flow
Design consent toggle UI variants (3 options: inline, modal, bottom-sheet)
Build Consent Ledger backend microservice and staging environment
gates: Backend audit API passes penetration testing

activities: Launch 3 UI variants + control to 5% of new users in Bangalore & Mumbai only
Monitor VCCR, onboarding completion, and marketing opt-in daily
Run qualitative usability tests with 12 participants per variant (Priya + Ramesh personas)
Iterate on copy and toggle placement based on session recordings
gates: No P0 bugs for 7 consecutive days

activities: Expand to 25% of new users across 8 cities (metros + Tier-2)
Activate Guardian Consent flow for age-gated cohorts
Stress-test Consent Ledger at 10× current event volume
Deploy in-app 'Privacy Center' v1 for consent revocation and data download
100% new user traffic gets SARAL onboarding
Launch 'Re-consent Campaign' for existing MAU via in-app banners + WhatsApp
Enable automated data erasure (Right to Erasure) workflow in Privacy Center
Publish transparency report: 'How Swiggy Protects Your Data'
gates: Data Protection Board mock audit: zero findings

testable_elements: JIT prompt timing (address entry vs. checkout vs. post-first-order)
Toggle UI pattern (stacked checkboxes vs. toggle switches vs. bottom-sheet cards)
Incentive framing for marketing opt-in (₹50 coupon vs. 'Personalized offers' vs. 'Support local restaurants')
Vernacular language variants (formal vs. colloquial Hindi/Tamil)
non_testable_compliance_elements: Essential data notice CANNOT be A/B tested with a 'no-notice' variant — this is legally mandated
Children's age gate CANNOT be bypassed in any variant
Consent Ledger logging CANNOT be disabled for any cohort
ab_testing_framework.handling_non_testable: For mandatory elements, we run 'compliance shadow tests' — show the new notice to 100% of a small cohort and measure behavioral impact vs. a holdback that sees the OLD compliant notice (not a non-compliant one). We never test illegality.

validation_methods: Quantitative: Funnel analysis, cohort retention, VCCR trend
Qualitative: 1:1 moderated usability tests in Hindi, Tamil, and Kannada
Legal: Mock audit by external DPDP consultant every 2 weeks
Technical: Chaos engineering on Consent Ledger (simulate Kafka outage, verify fallback queue)

slides.speaker_notes: Phase 1 is the make-or-break moment. We do NOT test whether compliance is needed — we test HOW to make compliance delightful. The non-testable list is our red line. We never put user rights up for experimentation.

slides.title: Risks & Trade-offs
slides.subtitle: Honest accounting of what we are trading for compliance

risks.risk: Consent Ledger microservice, Kafka pipelines, and immutable storage add significant backend surface area.
risks.mitigation: Start with managed Kafka (Confluent Cloud) and WORM S3. Allocate 2 senior backend engineers full-time for 8 weeks. Build fallback to in-memory queue if Ledger is down.
risks.trade_off: We accept 3-week backend timeline extension to avoid a lifetime of legal liability.

risks.risk: Unbundling consent will reduce immediate marketing opt-in from 100% to ~45%. This impacts ad revenue and cross-sell LTV in the short term.
risks.mitigation: Deploy JIT incentives (₹50 Instamart coupon for opt-in). Use first-party data from essential processing (order history) for limited personalization even without explicit marketing consent. Re-engage dormant opt-ins via quarterly value prompts.
risks.trade_off: We trade 6 months of lower ad revenue for a sustainable, legally defensible monetization model.

risks.risk: Additional taps and screens during onboarding may increase drop-off, especially for Tier-2/3 users and low-literacy segments.
risks.mitigation: Progressive disclosure: only essential notices at signup; optional toggles deferred to JIT moments. Optimize for <30-second total consent time. Provide 'Quick Start' mode that auto-denies optional streams and lets user proceed instantly.
risks.trade_off: We trade a small amount of top-of-funnel conversion for dramatically improved trust and long-term retention.

risks.category: Edge Case: User Declines All Optional Consent
risks.risk: User disables location, marketing, analytics, and third-party sharing. App must still function perfectly for core delivery.
risks.mitigation: Engineering must build 'minimal mode' where: (1) Location falls back to manual address entry; (2) Marketing is fully suppressed; (3) Analytics uses only aggregated, non-identifiable events; (4) Third-party sharing is blocked at API gateway level.
risks.trade_off: We accept higher engineering cost for 'privacy mode' to guarantee DPDP compliance for every user archetype.

risks.category: Edge Case: Child User with Non-Cooperative Guardian
risks.risk: Parent refuses consent or ignores OTP. Child cannot use Swiggy, leading to negative word-of-mouth.
risks.mitigation: Guardian Consent Mode includes: (1) Clear explanation of data minimization for child accounts; (2) 'Limited Access' option allowing order placement with zero data retention beyond transaction; (3) Educational content for parents on digital safety.
risks.trade_off: We accept losing some under-18 user volume to avoid DPDP Section 9 penalties (up to ₹250 crore).

risks.category: Edge Case: Legacy User Data Backfill
risks.risk: 24M existing users gave bundled consent. We cannot prove their consent was valid under DPDP.
risks.mitigation: Launch 'Trust Refresh' campaign: in-app banner + WhatsApp + email asking users to re-consent granularly. For non-responders, downgrade data usage to 'essential only' and purge behavioral profiles older than 12 months. Document 'legitimate use' basis for historical transaction data.
risks.trade_off: We accept a temporary reduction in data richness for legacy users to cleanse our legal liability.

slides.speaker_notes: This is the slide where we prove we are not naive. We know conversion will hurt. We know engineering is hard. We know legacy data is a mess. But the cost of inaction is existential. The trade-offs are worth it because the alternative is regulatory shutdown of our data operations.

slides.title: Conclusion: Privacy as a Product, Not a Penalty
slides.subtitle: Next steps and the decision we need today

content.summary: Swiggy SARAL transforms DPDP compliance from a legal burden into a competitive advantage. By unbundling consent, deploying just-in-time micro-notices, building an immutable Consent Ledger, and protecting children's data, we achieve three goals simultaneously: (1) Full legal compliance, (2) Preserved core conversion, (3) Enhanced brand trust.

three_pillars.achievement: Zero bundled consent. Verifiable ledger. Parental gateway. Audit-ready in <2 minutes.
Core onboarding unblocked. Marketing opt-in recovered via value-driven JIT. Premium trust positioning.
Plain-language, multilingual notices. One-tap revocation. Transparent data usage.

content.decision_ask: Approve the following today: (1) Resource allocation: 2 senior backend engineers + 1 designer + 1 legal PM for 12 weeks; (2) Budget: ₹45 lakhs for translation, audit, and managed infrastructure; (3) Go/No-Go for Phase 1 A/B experiment launch on July 20, 2026.

immediate_next_steps.action: Finalize PRD for Consent Ledger microservice and JIT trigger logic
Deploy staging environment for SARAL backend + frontend variants
Complete DPIA and submit to Data Protection Officer for sign-off
Deliver high-fidelity prototypes for 3 UI variants + vernacular copy decks
Define incentive framework for marketing JIT opt-in (coupon budget + terms)

content.closing_statement: The DPDP Act is not a deadline to fear. It is a blueprint for building the most trusted food-tech platform in India. Let's build Swiggy SARAL — because our users' data belongs to them, and their trust belongs to us.

slides.speaker_notes: End with energy and clarity. The ask is specific: people, money, and a date. Everyone in the room should know exactly what they own by the time they leave. Privacy is not a cost center. It is the product.
